task: I’m dealing with a rehab contractor who wants to change the work order after the schedule has already been set, and it’s starting to affect the timeline and payment milestones. What’s the best way to handle the conversation and keep the project moving?

turns.agent_response:
Best approach: keep it calm, document-driven, and tied to the contract.

### 1) Get clear on what’s changing
Ask the contractor to specify:
- exactly what work they want changed
- why it’s needed
- how it affects cost, timeline, and milestones

If it’s not in writing, treat it as informal discussion only.

### 2) Refer back to the approved scope and schedule
Say something like:
> “We agreed to the current scope, schedule, and payment milestones. If you’re requesting a change, please submit it as a written change order with the impact on cost and completion dates.”

That keeps the conversation professional and anchored to the agreement.

### 3) Don’t approve changes verbally
If you let them proceed without documentation, it can create disputes later over:
- extra charges
- delays
- whether milestones were met
- who approved what

### 4) Require a formal change order
A good change order should include:
- description of the change
- added/deleted work
- revised price
- revised schedule
- effect on payment milestones
- signatures from both parties

No signed change order, no scope change.

### 5) Protect the timeline
If the change is legitimate and you want to keep moving:
- approve only the specific change
- keep the rest of the schedule intact
- set a new deadline for the revised work
- make clear that unrelated milestones should still be met

### 6) Tie payments to completed work
If milestones are being affected, confirm in writing that payment is only due for:
- completed work
- work accepted under the original scope
- approved change-order work

### 7) Keep communication factual
Avoid emotional back-and-forth. Use short, professional messages:
> “We’re happy to review any requested scope changes. Please send the change order request with pricing and schedule impact so we can evaluate it promptly.”

### 8) Escalate if needed
If the contractor keeps pushing without documentation:
- remind them of the contract terms
- pause approval of extra work
- consider involving your project manager, attorney, or lender if applicable

If you want, I can help you draft a firm but professional email or text response to the contractor.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s basically the right play. Keep it on paper, not in a hallway conversation. If they want to change scope after the schedule is set, they submit a written change order with cost, time, and milestone impact. Until it’s signed, nothing moves.

What I’d add: don’t just say “please send it.” Tie it to payment. Something like: current draw stays on original scope only, and any extra work is outside the existing milestone. That cuts the games off fast.

Also, if the change is real but they’re using it to buy time, separate the issue. Approve the minimum needed to keep the job moving, but don’t let them rewrite the whole sequence. I’ve had contractors try this when they were behind. Once you let them blur scope and schedule together, you lose leverage.

If you want, I can give you a short email that’s firm without sounding messy.
